nativeRange.habitat: Marine and reef-associated, found across a depth range of roughly 2 to 61 m (occasionally to 90 m), most commonly below 10 m on deeper lagoon slopes, channels, and seaward reef faces.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with crevices and overhangs
open swimming space across the mid-water column
subdued lighting near refuges where the fish can retreat

diet.notes: In the wild, the Orange Butterflyfish is generally considered to feed mainly on soft coral polyps — notably those of Sarcophyton and Litophyton species — along with algae and zooplankton. In aquaria, it has been reported to accept a wide variety of meaty and prepared marine foods, including mysis shrimp, finely chopped seafood, marine flake and pellet, and frozen marine preparations. It has also been observed eating Aiptasia pest anemones in captivity. Feeding at least two to three times per day is generally advised, particularly during the initial acclimation period.

goodWith: dwarf and large angelfishes (in suitably large systems)
avoidWith: soft corals (Sarcophyton, leathers — at risk of polyp nipping)
LPS corals and zoanthids in many cases
very aggressive triggers or large dottybacks that stress the fish
conspecifics outside of an established mated pair

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 5696x3797. Tack-sharp full-body side profile of a vibrant golden-yellow individual with the diagnostic blue-black eye-band, black lips, and pale-blue scale spotting against clean teal water. Most striking, best-lit specimen.
Jenny (JennyHuang) from Taipei; 2245x1523. Sharp full side profile with vivid blue eye-band, black snout/lips and golden body set against a red gorgonian; correct ID, striking color contrast.

Diego Delso; 4887x3258. Sharp full-body shot of a strongly orange-toned individual showing the black eye-band, white face and spotted flank pattern; good alternate morph matching the Orange Butterflyfish trade name.

summary: A peaceful, wide-ranging Indo-Pacific butterflyfish, generally golden-yellow with a dark eye-band. Among Chaetodon species, it is commonly considered one of the more approachable in terms of feeding and hardiness. Generally not considered fully reef-safe, as it tends to nip soft coral polyps in many systems.
careNotes:
The Orange Butterflyfish (Chaetodon kleinii) is one of the most widely distributed members of its family, ranging across the Indo-West Pacific from the Red Sea and East Africa east to Hawaii and Samoa, north to southern Japan and south to New South Wales, with an additional disjunct population in the Galapagos. In the wild it is commonly encountered below 10 m on deeper lagoon slopes, channels, and seaward reefs, typically in pairs or small groups, and is assessed as IUCN Least Concern.

It is commonly considered one of the more approachable butterflyfishes for aquarium keeping: it tends to accept prepared foods more readily than most members of the genus Chaetodon and is generally regarded as fairly hardy once settled. Even so, like other butterflyfishes it benefits from a mature aquarium with stable water chemistry, plenty of live rock for refuge, and open mid-water swimming space. Aquarium temperature recommendations vary between sources, and the ranges given here represent a practical middle ground.

Reef compatibility is mixed. The species feeds mainly on soft coral polyps in the wild — notably Sarcophyton tracheliophorum and Litophyton viridis — and is widely considered not fully reef-safe as a result. At the same time, it has been observed consuming Aiptasia — a common nuisance anemone — in captivity, and individuals in mixed reef systems have been noted to graze on them opportunistically. A fish-only or FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) system is the safer choice if prized soft corals are present.

Like most butterflyfishes, the Orange Butterflyfish has been documented forming monogamous pairs during breeding, and established pairs should not be split. Coloration in the trade is variable: individuals can range from pale golden-yellow to more brownish tones, and the dark eye-band can appear black or black-and-blue, which accounts for many of its trade names, including Sunburst, Klein's, Brown, Blacklip, and Corallicola Butterflyfish.
breedingNotes: The Orange Butterflyfish reproduces by releasing eggs and sperm into open water (pelagic spawning), and forms monogamous pairs during breeding. It has not been reliably bred in home aquaria, and trade specimens are essentially all wild-collected.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Chaetodontidae, Acanthuriformes), maximum length (15.0 cm TL), Indo-West Pacific distribution, depth range (0-90 m, usually below 10 m), wild temperature range (24.7-29 °C, mean 27.8 °C), wild diet (soft coral polyps, algae and zooplankton) and reproductive mode (oviparous, monogamous, pair-bonding).

additional.name: reefapp.net
additional.notes: Additional cross-check on adult size (15 cm), peaceful temperament, hardiness/care level, and reef compatibility (described as 'reef safe with luck' — most specimens nip corals/inverts). Tank-size minimum given as 300 L; combined with LiveAquaria's 454 L, the larger value was preferred and rounded to 500 L per the playbook tank-size rule.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used only for taxonomy and synonym cross-check (family, order, subgenus Lepidochaetodon, common-name synonyms) and to confirm Aiptasia-eating behavior in captivity.

fish.breedingNotes: Releases eggs and sperm into open water (pelagic spawning) and forms monogamous pairs during breeding. Not known to have been successfully reared in home aquaria.
